subject: eddy-lazzarin

sectors_excited.name: Stablecoins & payments
sectors_excited.why: Stablecoins have hit clear product-market fit — they're the cheapest way to move a dollar, and after this year's L2 fee collapse the unit economics finally work for real payments, not just on-chain collateral. Payments was always one of the original goals; the pieces are now in place, and I expect enterprises and SMBs to start switching off card rails.

citations.quote: Payments has been one of the goals of crypto from the beginning. The pieces are finally coming together.

sectors_excited.name: Zero-knowledge / SNARKs / verifiable compute
sectors_excited.why: Proving overhead is dropping from ~10^9 toward 10^6, which moves SNARKs from theory into practice. With Jolt/Lasso and Binius on the roadmap, this unlocks scaling, privacy, and verifiable compute (including for AI and content provenance). Decades-old research is accelerating because of crypto.

citations.quote: Today we're sharing our biggest open source release yet: Jolt Alpha, our zkVM built new from the ground up.

sectors_excited.name: AI + crypto (autonomous agents)
sectors_excited.why: AI is how machines think; crypto is how machines own. Agents can't have bank accounts but they can have wallets — they'll transact, pay each other, and pay for their own compute in a verifiably autonomous way. It's the most novel design space opening up, and it needs crypto rails plus identity/auth between agents.

citations.quote: agents being able to pay each other, and even for their own compute, is what I had in mind here. It will definitely happen.

sectors_excited.name: Proof of personhood / private decentralized identity
sectors_excited.why: As AI drives the marginal cost of fake content and fake identities toward zero, we need privacy-preserving, unique (Sybil-resistant) proof of personhood. The point isn't to prove a person exists — it's to raise the cost of attacks without raising costs for real people. A non-negotiable building block for a trustworthy web, and it wants a credibly-neutral blockchain underneath.

citations.quote: How bad does this need to get before all networks need to integrate a private proof of personhood system like @worldnetwork?

sectors_excited.name: Fully on-chain games
sectors_excited.why: Games push software's envelope and are already the highest-throughput thing on-chain. When no one in particular controls the game server, modding and composability become first-class — third parties can extend the world without breaking it. I've expected on-chain games to fill the Zachtronics-style niche, and the cheap blockspace to support it is finally arriving.

citations.quote: Narrative violation: #1 chain measured by Mgas/s (the correct measure for throughput) is @ProofOfPlay — by a long shot.
I believe fully on-chain games will fill a similar niche in coming years.

sectors_excited.name: L2 scaling & blockchain infrastructure
sectors_excited.why: Radically cheaper, high-quality blockspace is the prerequisite for everything else — payments, micro-priced NFTs, on-chain games, complex markets. L2 fees collapsed after EIP-4844 and throughput is up ~50x in four years. Light clients (Helios for L2s) are becoming critical for rollup interoperability. Founders pick infra on expected future capacity, so this has to keep accelerating.

citations.quote: Ethereum cannot afford to slow down.

sectors_concerned.name: Memecoins / pure speculation (the casino)
sectors_concerned.why: Memecoins are explicitly purposeless — gambling, not protocols. Interest comes and goes, but the casino sets the industry back: it deters great founders, hands politicized regulators ammunition, and frames crypto as a risky casino rather than a new computing platform. They're also not technically interesting, so they don't attract builders. I see the damage every day.

citations.quote: Memecoins alter how the public, regulators, and entrepreneurs see crypto.
Besides undermining the long-term vision of crypto that has kept so many of us in the space, memecoins aren't very technically interesting.

tokens_excited.name: ETH (Ethereum)
tokens_excited.why: Not a price call — I think in protocols, not pie charts. But ETH is the rare network token with a real economic loop: issuance pays a security budget to validators, and EIP-1559 burns capture value when blockspace is consumed. It's still the clear builder leader and the L2 ecosystem compounds its network effects. My one worry is complacency on scaling.
tokens_excited.age_note: long-held conviction in Ethereum's network-token economics and leadership; settlement-growth framing stated 2021-02, not restated as a price view

citations.quote: In one year, Ethereum has grown from settling ~$373m per day to over $9b per day.

risk_regime.why: I read markets through the price-innovation cycle, not sentiment — trust the instruments (fundamentals), not the herd. Right now both are unusually aligned: ETP approvals, real regulatory momentum, L2 fees collapsed, activity at all-time highs, and builder energy clearly back. It's still early, so I'm leaning in and building aggressively. The 'risk-on' is conviction in the technology, not a short-term price prediction.
risk_regime.age_note: core framework (price-innovation cycle, 'still early'), applied to the end-2024 moment

notes: Lens caveat: I'm an engineer/builder, not a trader, and I deliberately avoid specific token-price bets — tokens are the native asset class of information networks, so I evaluate them as protocols (goals, mechanisms, faucets/sinks), not as pie charts. Hence rich sector views and a single, carefully-framed token view. Skeptical in analysis, optimistic in action.
